coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on carbon dots and multicolor CdTe quantum dots for multiplexed determination of heavy metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yuanyuan
Wang, Yongbo
Mao, Gennian
Liang, Chengyuan
Fan, Min
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the high risk to human and environmental health, heavy metal pollution has become a global problem. Rapid, accurate and multiplexed determination of heavy metal ions is critical. In this work, we reported a promising approach to designing ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes for multiplexed determination of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions. The nanoprobes (CDs-QDx) were designed by mixing the CDs and multicolor CdTe QDs without the involvement of recognition elements. The CDs were insensitive to heavy metal ions while CdTe QDs showed the size-dependent fluorescence response to different heavy metal ions, thereby establishing a ratiometric detection scheme by measuring the fluorescence intensity ratios of CDs-QDx systems. By evaluating the detection performance, the CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) were successfully used for differentiation and quantification of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions. In addition, we also carried out the detection of heavy metal ions in actual samples with acceptable results. We believed that this work offers new insight into the design of ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for multiplexed determination of not only heavy metals but also some other analytes by combining the CDs with CdTe QDs with fine-tuned sizes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes
Heavy metal ions
Colormetric response
Multiplexed determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CdTe QDs
The differentiation of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions by visualization
Detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions
The characterization of the CDs and CdTe QDs
The fluorescence response of CDs and QDs to heavy metal ions
Mechanism of fluorescence response of QDs to heavy metal ions
Assembly of the ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes
Differentiation of three heavy metal ions
The ratiometric fluorescent detection of heavy metals

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 32001783 ]; the Science and Technology Program of Shaanxi Province [grant number 2019JQ-484 , 2021NY-123]; the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department [grant number 20JK0540 ]; and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi University of Science and Technology [grant number 2018BJ-53 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs (a) and CdTe QDs (b). (c) The FT-IR spectroscopy of CDs, CdTe QDs, and the mixture of CDs and CdTe QDs. (d) The zeta potentials of CDs (1), QD570 (2), QD650 (3), QD702 (4), CDs-QD570 (5), CDs-QD650 (6), and CDs-QD702 (7).
The fluorescence response of the CDs (0.11 M) for various metal ions. The concentrations of the metal ions were all 10 μM.

The fluorescence response of QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) (0.2 mM) for Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag + ions. I0 is the fluorescence intensity without the heavy metal ions, I is the fluorescence intensity with different concentrations of heavy metal ions.

The absorption spectra of QD570 (a), QD650 (b), and QD702 (c) without and with the heavy metal ions. The fluorescence lifetimes of QD570 (d), QD650 (e), and QD702 (f) without and with the heavy metal ions.
The fluorescence spectra of CDs, QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702), and CDs-QDx.

The fluorescence response of CDs-QD570 (a), CDs-QD650 (b), and CDs-QD702 (c) for various metal ions, the concentrations of the metal ions were all 10 μM. Inset: the corresponding samples under UV lamp (365 nm).

(a) Color patterns of CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) in the presence of different heavy metal ions. The concentrations of heavy metal ions （Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+） in the CDs-QD570, CDs-QD650, and CDs-QD702 were 300 nM, 1000 nM, and 300 nM, respectively. (b) Simulated digitalized color pattern corresponding to (a). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions. The photographs of the CDs-QD570 (a), CDs-QD650 (d), and CDs-QD702 (g) in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions under UV lamp (365 nm), respectively. The fluorescence spectra of CDs-QD570 (b), CDs-QD650 (e), and CDs-QD702 (h) in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) and the concentrations of Hg2+ (c), Cu2+ (f), and Ag+ (i) ions.
(a) Presentation of the synthesis procedure of CDs and CdTe QDs and the design of the ratiometric sensing systems. (b) The process of ratiometric fluorescent detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions.
Comparison of the reported fluorescent probes for the determination of heavy metal ions.
Recoveries of samples spiked with heavy metal ions by CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702).

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on carbon dots and multicolor CdTe quantum dots for multiplexed determination of heavy metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to the high risk to human and environmental health, heavy metal pollution has become a global problem. Rapid, accurate and multiplexed determination of heavy metal ions is critical. In this work, we reported a promising approach to designing ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes for multiplexed determination of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions. The nanoprobes (CDs-QDx) were designed by mixing the CDs and multicolor CdTe QDs without the involvement of recognition elements. The CDs were insensitive to heavy metal ions while CdTe QDs showed the size-dependent fluorescence response to different heavy metal ions, thereby establishing a ratiometric detection scheme by measuring the fluorescence intensity ratios of CDs-QDx systems. By evaluating the detection performance, the CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) were successfully used for differentiation and quantification of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions. In addition, we also carried out the detection of heavy metal ions in actual samples with acceptable results. We believed that this work offers new insight into the design of ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for multiplexed determination of not only heavy metals but also some other analytes by combining the CDs with CdTe QDs with fine-tuned sizes.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions, even at trace levels, have been detected in different sources including natural waters, plants, soil, food, etc. The use of pesticides and fertilizers, burning of fossil fuels, and mining are all sources of heavy metal contamination [1]. Among these heavy metals, mercury (Hg) is recognized as a most hazardous pollution and a strong carcinogen, which can enter the food chain directly from contaminated water and bioaccumulates [2,3]. Copper (Cu) plays an important role in the physiological and pathological processes of the human body. However, excessive level of Cu2+ disrupts the balance between cells, which can lead to neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's [4,5]. Silver (Ag), a heavy metal that comes from industrial waste gas and water, may lead to silver poisoning, liver damage, and kidney damage [6,7]. Given the public health problems caused by multiple heavy metals that co-exist in the environment, developing a sensitive and efficient analytical method for the multiplexed detection of low concentrations of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions in real samples is urgently needed.

At present, traditional approaches for quantitatively analyzing trivial amounts of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), atomic emission spectroscopy (AES), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), electrochemical method, ion chromatography, and other detection techniques have been well-developed [3,7,8]. These routine techniques can meet the requirements of accuracy and sensitivity, which can be successfully employed to detect trace quantity levels of metals in many relevant samples. However, they can only achieve the determination of one specific type of metal ions and suffer from some limitations, such as high cost, time-consuming, tedious sample preparation, expensive setting, and well-trained operators, which limit their applications [9–11].

Compared with traditional detection methods, fluorescence analysis has the advantages of high efficiency, portability, and high detection sensitivity, so it is an alternative method to solve these problems [12,13]. The fluorescent probe-based methods have drawn more and more attention because of the properties of high sensitivity, non-destructive, quickness, simplicity and real-time visual detection of trace metals in related samples [14–17]. However, one of the biggest challenges of conventional fluorescent probes is their absolute intensity-dependent signal readout, which causes inaccurate sensing results due to the presence of various analyte-independent factors that can cause fluctuations in their absolute signal intensity. In contrast, the ratiometric fluorescent probe, which generally employs the ratio of two isolated emission peaks of different wavelengths by self-calibration, has provided more accurate measurement. Because of the built-in reference signal, it can reduce systematic errors, including the fluctuation of detection conditions and the background fluorescence [18,19].

As we know, for the general sensing methods, antibodies, antigens, aptamers, DNA, and enzymes have been widely utilized as recognition elements to enhance the selectivity of the sensors for the detection of target analytes. However, the combination of the QDs with different recognition elements increases the experimental complexity and cost, and the procedure also changes the optical properties, reducing the reproducibility of the sensing results [20,21].

In this work, we have, for the first time, developed a proof-of-concept on a double QD based ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy, which was based on the CDs and multicolor CdTe QDs without the involvement of recognition elements (such as aptamers, molecularly imprinted polymer, antibodies, DNA, and enzymes) (Scheme 1 a). The principle of the designed ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes was based on the size-dependent fluorescence response of QDs to different target analytes. By the combination of CDs and different sized CdTe QDs, the differentiation and quantitation of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions were achieved (Scheme 1b). It is worth mentioning that the proposed ratiometric sensing systems could be prepared by only mixing the CDs and different sized CdTe QDs without complicated synthesizing procedure. Moreover, except for multiplexed determination of multiple heavy metal ions, we believe that this strategy offers new insight for the design of ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe and can be used for detecting some other analytes by tuning the QD sizes in the sensing systems.

All chemicals are at least analytical reagent and used without additional purification. Tellurium granule (Te) and 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycine, Cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O), and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (30% H2O2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and anhydrous ethanol were obtained from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) buffer solutions were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Stock solutions of Hg2+ (1.0 mM) were prepared in acidic solution (HCl), and Cu2+and Ag + solution of 1.0 mM was prepared with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by an improved one-pot hydrothermal method [22]. Briefly, 1 g of glycine and 400 μL of H2O2 (30%wt) were dissolved in 25 mL deionized water under vigorous stirring, then the pH value of the mixture was adjusted to 8 with 1.0 M NaOH solution. The resultant solution was transferred in a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and the temperature was held at 180 °C for 2 h. The reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the color of the solution turned from colorless to dark yellow. Then, the obtained CDs solution was further purified by dialysis against deionized water for 12 h to move the excess reactants. Finally, the aqueous solution of purified CDs was stored for further use (the concentration of CD solution was calculated according to the concentration of glycine).

The water-soluble 3-MPA-capped CdTe QDs were synthesized according to the reference with a minor modification [9]. Firstly, 0.2284 g (1 mmol) of CdCl2·2.5H2O and 210 μL (∼2 mmol) of mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water and the pH was adjusted to 10 with NaOH solution (1.0 M) under stirring at 60 °C. Secondly, Te powder ground from Te granule (0.3062 g, 2.4 mmol) were put into a 250 mL three-neck round bottom flask and reacted with excessive NaBH4 (0.35 g) in 24 mL deionized water at 40 °C. When the clear purple solution produced, the NaHTe was obtained. Then, 10 mL of freshly prepared NaHTe solution was injected into the Cd2+ precursor solution, and the mixture was heated to 100 °C (final molar ratio, Cd:Te:MPA = 1:2.4:2) and the CdTe nanocrystals were immediately formed with a color change from colorless into orange. And then the 75 mL of mixture was added into a Teflon autoclave, which was tightly sealed and maintained at 160 °C. The CdTe QDs with emission at 570 nm, 650 nm, and 702 nm could be obtained in 5 min, 10 min, and 15 min and were denoted as QD570, QD650, and QD702, respectively. The purification of the QDs solution was conducted by dialyzing it for 8 h. The purified CdTe QDs were dispersed in deionized water for the next use (the concentrations of CdTe QDs were calculated according to the concentration of precursor Cd).

The ratiometric fluorescent sensing systems were designed by mixing the CDs and different sized CdTe QDs (QD570, QD650, and QD702) and the corresponding systems were used as ratiometric nanoprobes and denoted as CDs-QD570, CDs-QD650, and CDs-QD702, respectively. Hereafter this text will be abbreviated as CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702). The fluorescence intensity ratios of the sensing system were controlled by the concentrations of the CDs (0.53 M) and CdTe QDs (1 mM). The volume ratios of CD and QDx in CDs-QDx systems were 2:1, 1:1, and 5:1, respectively.

The fluorescence response of the nanoprobes for different metals (Cd2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, and Mn2+) were investigated. The ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) with the fluorescence intensity ratio of 1:2 was uniformly incubated with the same concentration (10 μM) of different metal ions in PBS buffer at room temperature. After ∼20 min, the fluorescence spectra of all samples were obtained and the photographs of fluorescence color under UV lamp were also obtained by a mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The differentiation of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions by visualization
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) for Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions were recorded under UV lamp (365 nm). First, 200 μL of probe CDs-QD570 was added to the 2 mL centrifuge tube, and then Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions solutions with the same concentration (300 nM) were added. Finally, the volume was fixed to 1 mL with PBS buffer. After 20 min, the fluorescence color of each sample could be observed with the naked eye under the UV lamp (λex = 365 nm), and the photographs were obtained by a mobile phone. In the same way, the fluorescence color of CDs-QD650 and CDs-QD702 with Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions were also recorded separately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: For Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions detection, 200 μL CDs-QDx probe and different concentrations of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions were dispersed in a centrifuge tube, respectively, and then diluted to 1 mL with PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The CDs-QD570, CDs-QD650, and CDs-QD702 were used as ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes for detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions, respectively. After the mixtures incubated for at least 20 min at room temperature, fluorescence spectra of the mixtures under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm with excitation and emission band-slits set as 2.0 nm were recorded. All the measurements were conducted at least in triplicate. The color changes were observed under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The standard addition method was used to evaluate the performance of the ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe in real samples. Here, the lake water and herb (Astragalus) were employed as real samples and the pretreatment were conducted before the spiking and recovery studies.

The lake water samples (from Keda Lake in Shaanxi University of Science and Technology) were filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane to remove large particles of impurities prior to analysis. The final solution was diluted with PBS at a 1:10 (v/v) ratio and used as lake water samples. The nanoprobe CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) were incubated with the samples spiked with various concentrations of heavy metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+) and the PL spectra were recorded in the same conditions as that in section 2.6. According to the results, the recoveries could be calculated.

The herb (Astragalus) was obtained from a local pharmaceutical factory. The herb samples were prepared as follow: the dry herb sample was ground and soaked in 50 mL deionized water, and then temperature was raised to 90 °C and stirred for 60 min. The mixture was first filtered twice using ordinary qualitative filter paper, and then the obtained solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane. The final solution was diluted with PBS at a 1:100 (v/v) ratio and used as herb samples. Finally, the nanoprobes were added in the herb samples spiked with different concentrations of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, and the PL spectra were recorded in the same conditions as that in section 2.6. According to the results, the recoveries could be calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of the CDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs and different sized CdTe QDs are shown in Fig. 1 . Under UV lamp (365 nm), the CDs showed strong blue fluorescence and the maximum emission wavelength was 460 nm. The different sized CdTe QDs showed distinctive color from yellow to dark red with emission peaks at ∼570 nm, ∼650 nm, and ∼702 nm, respectively. In addition, the TEM images showed the CDs and CdTe QDs were well dispersible and the average diameter of CDs was 16.14 nm and QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) were 2.65 nm, 3.38 nm, and 3.47 nm, respectively (Fig. S1).

As indicated by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis (Fig. 1c), the strong broad absorption band of O–H stretching mode at ∼3400 cm−1 and a sharp peak of CO at ∼1600 cm−1 strongly verified the existence of –OH and –COOH on the surface of CDs and CdTe QDs. The FT-IR of CDs-QDs was similar to that of CDs and CdTe QDs, indicating that there was no interaction between CDs and CdTe QDs. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were also carried out to identify the composition and valence states of CDs and CdTe QDs (Figs. S3 and S4), and the corresponding discussion was in Supplementary material. In addition, the zeta potential of CDs was −4.14 mV and the zeta potentials of QD570, QD650, and QD702 were −24.8 mV, −29.0 mV, and −27.3 mV, respectively (Fig. 1d). The zeta potential of the mixture of CDs and QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) were also measured and the values were −10.1 mV, −12.6 mV, and −13.9 mV, respectively. The zeta potential values indicated that there was no electrostatic attraction between CDs and CdTe QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence response of CDs and QDs to heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Under the UV lamp (365 nm), the CDs samples with different metal ions showed bright blue fluorescence without obvious enhancement or quenching. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs changed slightly and no red or blue shift of the fluorescence spectra were observed in the presence of different ions (Fig. 2 ). These results indicated that CDs were insensitive to these common metal ions, and the CDs, therefore, could act as a reference signal to allow the response signal to be normalized.

However, CdTe QDs synthesized without further surface modification showed obvious fluorescence response to some metal ions, especially heavy metal ions. According to our previous study and other reported literatures [23–26], the fluorescence response of QDs to the analytes were dependent on their sizes. To achieve the distinguishing detection of three kinds of heavy metal ions, the fluorescence response of QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) to Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions were recorded separately (Figs. S5–S7) and the results were discussed in detail.

For Hg2+ ions, the results indicated that smaller QDs (QD570) showed high quenching efficiency compared to larger ones (QD650 and QD702). The QD650 and QD702 showed obvious fluorescence quenching only when the concentration of Hg2+ ions was above 15 μM (Fig. 3 a). These results indicated that the smaller QDs were more sensitive to Hg2+ ions. The fluorescence response of QDs to Cu2+ ions was similar to that of Hg2+ ions. In low level of Cu2+ ions, QD570 showed obvious fluorescence quenching. Except for the fluorescence quenching, however, the shape of spectra and maximum emission wavelength of QD570 in the presence of Cu2+ ions were also changed. The fluorescence quenching of QD650 and QD702 were also observed in the higher concentration of Cu2+ ions while the shape of spectra and the maximum emission wavelength remained unchanged (Fig. 3b). Unlike Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, it was noteworthy that the fluorescence response of QDs to Ag+ ions was distinctive. The fluorescence of smaller QDs (QD570) increased in the presence of Ag+ ions, a phenomenon that not occurred in the case of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions (Fig. 3c). However, the larger QDs showed the opposite fluorescence response. With the increasing of Ag+ concentration, the fluorescence of QD650 and QD702 were quenched gradually. In addition, the results suggested the fluorescence of QD650 was more effectively quenched compared to QD702, confirming that smaller QDs were more sensitive to Ag+ ions. Significantly, the distinctive fluorescence response of QDs to Ag+ ions would provide a possibility to differentiate Ag+ ions from other heavy metal ions qualitatively.

Based on the above finding, we conclude that the fluorescence response of different sized CdTe QDs to one kind of heavy metal ion was different, and the fluorescence response of certain sized QDs to different heavy metal ions was also different, which provided a theoretical and experimental basis for the distinguishing detection of three heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence response of QDs to heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the mechanism of the fluorescence response of the nanoprobes to heavy metal ions, the fluorescence lifetime, absorbance spectra, and TEM images of CDs-QDx with and without heavy metal ions were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 , the absorption spectra of QDx were not changed while the fluorescence lifetimes of the QDx decreased after addition of heavy metal ions except Ag+ ions (the fluorescence lifetime increased). The results indicated that the fluorescence quenching of QDx in the presence of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions was a dynamic process [4,27].

As shown in Figs. S5–S7, the redshift in fluorescence spectra of QDs was observed in all the fluorescence spectra of QD570, QD650, and QD702 in the presence of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions, respectively. However, the degree of redshift was different in the cases of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions. The redshift of fluorescence was also supported by the observation of the TEM images with and without heavy metal ions. As shown in Fig. S2, the TEM images suggested that the heavy metal ions did not cause aggregation and the QD nanoparticles were still well dispersible. However, the sizes of QDs increased slightly after addition of heavy metal ions (the inset of Fig. S2). According to the references [6,28], the ligands (MPA) could detach from the surface of QDs due to the competition of ligands between heavy metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+) and MPA. Therefore, the increase in average QD sizes could be attributed to the fact that a small proportion of QD nanoparticles could merge with each other because of the lack of ligands on QD surface, however, it was not enough to cause obvious aggregation.

Some theories have been reported to explain the mechanism of ﬂuorescence quenching of QDs by metal ions, among which electron transfer may be the most suitable explanation [29,30]. Combined with the above results, we believed that the fluorescent quenching of QDs was due to the effective electron transfer between donor (QDs) and acceptors (Hg2+ and Cu2+) which induce the disruption of radiative electron-hole recombination. In addition, the different sized QDs showed different fluorescence response patterns. These results confirmed that the size of QDs could modulate the electron transfer efficiency. The smaller QDs showed higher affinity for heavy metal ions because the quencher can reach the nanoparticle core while for the larger ones the interaction with the excitons is restrained at the surface [31]. The absorption and emission spectra showed that the maximum emission wavelength of CDs was 460 nm and the absorption peaks of QD570, QD650, and QD702 were ∼530, ∼610, and ∼650 nm, respectively (Fig. 1a and b). Therefore, the inner-filter effect (IFE) could be ruled out because there was no spectral overlap between CDs and CDs.

Additionally, the size-dependent fluorescence response was also analyzed. As shown in Fig. 3, the fluorescence of QDx were all quenched in the presence of heavy metal ions except Ag+ ions. The results indicated that the smaller QDs showed high quenching efficiency. As discussed above, the electron transfer eliminates or quenches fluorescence from the QDs and therefore the size-dependent fluorescence quenching of QDs can be attributed to the numbers of quenchers (heavy metal ions) on the surface of QDs. In the case of the smaller QDs, the larger specific surface area causes enough surface sites, thereby facilitating the approach of quenchers to the QD surface [23]. Therefore, the QDs showed size-dependent fluorescence quenching in the presence of heavy metal ions.

However, the experimental results suggested that Ag+ ions was always unique among the three metal ions in terms of fluorescence response and fluorescence lifetimes. Further research will be carried out to investigate the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement and quenching caused by Ag+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of the ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the fluorescence response results above, we developed a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe in which CdTe QDs were used as the response signal and CDs as the reference signal, achieving the ratiometric detection and reducing the interference from various target-independent factors.

According to the above results, the zeta potentials of CDs and different sized CdTe QDs were all negative (Fig. 1d), which enable the sensing systems stable because they had no electrostatic attraction. In addition, there was also no IFE between CDs and QDs. Therefore, the fluorescence of the CDs and QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) remained nearly unchanged in the CDs-QDx systems (Fig. 5 ).

On the basis of the facts, we prepared the ratiometric nanoprobe systems by only mixing the CDs and different sized CdTe QDs according to a certain proportion. The fluorescence intensity ratio and the fluorescence color could be adjusted by the concentrations of CDs and QDx (Fig. S8). The corresponding ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe was denoted as CDs-QD570, CDs-QD650 and CDs-QD702, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratios between the fluorescence intensities of CDs and QDx were used as detection signals for detecting three heavy metals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of three heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above experimental results, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs were all declined in the presence of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions while the fluorescence intensity of QD570 was enhanced in the presence of Ag+ ions. Therefore, differentiation of the three heavy metal ions is an essential prerequisite for the distinguishing detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions.

The size-dependent fluorescence response to different heavy metal ions provided a possibility to reflect the difference of the three heavy metal ions, which enable the differentiation of three heavy metal ions by colorimetric method. First, to verify the selectivity for Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions, the fluorescence spectra of CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) for various metal ions were recorded (Fig. S9). As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of the samples with different metal ions remained nearly the same with the blank samples except Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag + ions. The fluorescence of QDx in the presence of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions were nearly entirely quenched while the fluorescence of CDs remained unchanged, realizing a visually recognizable color change from pink to blue.

The fluorescence of system CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) in the presence of three heavy metal ions were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence of each system in the presence of the Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions were obviously different with each other (under 365 UV lamp). Such size-dependent fluorescence response may act as the unique fingerprints for the differentiation of three heavy metal ions. To distinguish the color variations more clearly, the results were transferred to the matrix form by stimulated color pattern, leading to a unique colormetric response pattern for each metal (Fig. 7b). Thus, the differentiation of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions could be achieved by just comparing the fluorescence color from the matrix figure under UV lamp.

In addition, when Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions were present in samples at the same time, it could also be determined by obtaining the fluorescence response of CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) separately and then compared with each other according to the colormetric response pattern (Fig. 7b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescent detection of heavy metals
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the above finding, we ultimately realized that the CDs-QDx systems could be potentially used as ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes for the distinguishing detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions, and by changing the size of CdTe QDs in the system, the detection performance could be potentially optimized.

The results above showed that smaller CdTe QDs were more sensitive for Hg2+ ions. For high sensitivity, therefore, the nanoprobe CDs-QD570 was used for detecting Hg2+ ions. In the sensing system, QD570 were used as sensing section and CDs were used as reference, realizing the ratiometric detection of Hg2+ ions. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence intensity of QD570 at 570 nm progressively decreased while the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 460 nm remained constant. A good linear relationship was shown between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I460/II570) and the concentrations of Hg2+ ions (R2 = 0.997), and the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) for Hg2+ ions were calculated as 22 nM and 74.21 nM, respectively (Fig. 8b and c). In addition, the detection performance of CDs-QD650 and CDs-QD702 for Hg2+ ions were also investigated, the results suggested that the sensitivity of the two sensing systems was not satisfied (Figs. S10 and S11). Thus, CDs-QD570 showed high sensitivity for Hg2+ ions and were used as the ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of Hg2+ ions.

The fluorescence intensity of QDs was also quenched in the presence of Cu2+ ions. For QD570, however, the fluorescence was declined gradually, accompanied with the red-shift of the spectra, thereby causing the uncertainty to the detection of Cu2+ ions (Fig. S6). Therefore, nanoprobes CDs-QD650 and CDs-QD702 were used as ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of Cu2+ ions. By calculation, the system of CDs-QD650 showed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the concentration of Cu2+ ions from 0 to 2000 nM, and the LOD and LOQ were calculated as 57 nM and 192.04 nM, respectively (Fig. 8f). The results detected by CDs-QD650 system were more sensitive, and the linear range was wider than that of CDs-QD702 (Fig. S12). Therefore, CDs-QD650 were chosen for the ratiometric detection of Cu2+ ions.

The fluorescence intensity at 570 nm increased as the concentration of Ag + ions increased while the phenomenon was not observed for Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions (Fig. S7). The system of CDs-QD570 could be used as ratiometric fluorescent probe for Ag+ ions, but the regression coefficient was not very good in the range of 0–400 nM, which might be due to the interaction between QD570 and Ag+ ions that passivated the surface traps state of QD570 [32,33]. For QD650, not only the PL intensity at 650 nm decreased, but also the obvious red-shift was observed in the presence of Ag+ ions, which affect the accuracy of detection (Fig. S13). The fluorescence intensity of QD702 was also decreased while no red and blue-shift was observed (Fig. 8g). By calculation, there was a good linear regression between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I460/I702) and concentrations of Ag+ ions with superior regression coefficient (R2 = 0.994) in the range of 0–1000 nM, and the LOD and LOQ were calculated as 45 nM and 150.10 nM, respectively (Fig. 8h and i), demonstrating the high sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe for Ag+ ions.

The analytical performance of CDs-QDx systems were compared with previous reported works [34–48]. As shown in Table 1 , the performance parameters of CDs-QDx system in our work are comparable or better than those of reported fluorescence probes. In addition, the CDs-QDx systems have the built-in reference signal that can reduce systematic errors and can be prepared without complicated procedure, and the detection can be conveniently performed. These advantages indicated that the CDs-QDx systems have high potential for practical use.

Significantly, if the Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions were present at the same time, in order to detect heavy metal ions accurately, chelating agents should be employed. According to previous study [6], the chelating agents, sodium diethyldithiocarbamate (DDTC), NH4OH, and 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN), were used to eliminate the interference from non-target heavy metal ions. As shown in Fig. S14, by using chelating agents of DDTC for Ag+ and Cu2+, NH4OH for Hg2+and Ag+ and PAN for Hg2+ and Cu2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ could be detected in coexistence with other two ions, respectively. Therefore, it demonstrated that the CDs-QDx systems were effective fluorescent sensing platform for multiplexed determination of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions.

Having illustrated the detection performance of the designed ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) for highly sensitive and distinguishing detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions in aqueous solution, we finally explored the ratiometric detection feasibility of the nanoprobe in real samples. Here, we employed lake water and herb as real sample models. Briefly, the ratiometric nanoprobe CDs-QDx and varying amounts of heavy metal ions were incubated in the real samples by the standard addition method. As exhibited in Table 2 , the acceptable recoveries ranging from 83.38% to 119.35% (the recoveries of Ag+ ions in herb samples not listed in Table 2) and the low relative standard deviation (RSD) (0.88%–6.18%) clearly suggested the good accuracy of the ratiometric nanoprobes for detection of three heavy metal ions in the real samples. Based on the results, we concluded that the designed ratiometric nanoprobes were highly applicable for the accurate detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions in real samples, which could lead to applications in safeguarding food and in environmental safety with the merits of speed, low price, and high sensitivity.

However, further research is needed to address some issues. For example, since the ratiometric nanoprobes were designed by mixing the CDs and CdTe QDs without interaction, such as covalent bond or electrostatic attraction, heterogeneous and unequal distributions of the CDs and different sized QDs in some complex matrix may result in false sensing results. In addition, the detection limits of our proposed nanoprobes are not very low. Nevertheless, in terms of the ratiometric sensing strategy in our work, the detection performance of the nanoprobes can be potentially optimized by selecting the QDs with fine-tuned sizes in further research.

In summary, we have demonstrated that CDs-QDx (x = 570, 650, and 702) systems could be employed as ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes for differentiation and sensitive detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions. The results indicated that CDs were insensitive to heavy metal ions while the different sized CdTe QDs showed size-dependent fluorescence response. Therefore, CdTe QDs were used as the response signal and CDs as the reference signal, achieving the ratiometric detection. By the unique colormetric response pattern of CDs-QDx for each type of metal ions, Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions could be differentiated visually. Considering the linear regression coefficient and LOD, the CDs-QD570, CDs-QD650, and CDs-QD702 were determined for detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions, respectively. Moreover, we explored the ratiometric sensing feasibility of the proposed nanoprobes for real samples detection and the results showed the acceptable recoveries. It should be worth mentioning that the ratiometric sensing systems could be prepared by combination the CDs and different sized CdTe QDs without the involvement of the recognition elements. Therefore, this work offers a new insight into ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe from the viewpoints of both preparation and detection mechanism.

Yuanyuan He: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Yongbo Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Gennian Mao: Project administration, Validation. Chengyuan Liang: Investigation, Resources, Formal analysis. Min Fan: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to inﬂuence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 32001783]; the Science and Technology Program of Shaanxi Province [grant number 2019JQ-484, 2021NY-123]; the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department [grant number 20JK0540]; and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi University of Science and Technology [grant number 2018BJ-53].
